metadata.title:
COUNCILMAN BROWNS IDEA
OF RECONSTRUCTlNG AND
ENLARGlNG CIVIC lEGlSLA-
TIVE BODY.

pages.raw_text:
Councilman L. c. Brown, of the
First ward, believes the city can
and should be redistricted on a
npartizanship basis, and also
sees no reason why the council
is barred from selecting the new
councilmen comprehended in pres
ent plans. The councils authority
in this regard has been brought
into question on conflicting charter
provisions, and Corporation Coun-
sci Hamblen is now preparing an
opinion on the points involved. In
speaking of the democratic attitude
toward redistricting Mr.. Brown has
the following to say:


THE question Of party control
could not, and moreover would not,
be changed by the passage of re.
districting measure, which has not
even been framed yet, and will not
be until the committee has made
it's report. When that committee
passes upon the ACCESSIBILITY of re-
districting | believe the result OF
their opinion will show that the
democrats do not desire to steal
the legislative body or thwart the
will of the people by a cnange or
the complexion of that body po-
litically. There is one thing GER
tain if the action taken now, and
which the democrats wish to carry
out, is in any way partisan, any fu'
ture action after the change of the
complexion of the council next
May with the republicans FREEDOM
mating, would be much more so, as
it is believed that party would take
unto themselves the naming of all
councilmen of their own political
faith. All that is wished by the
members of the council as at pres
ent constituted, and especially the
democrats, Is to meet the neces-
stiles of our rapidly growing city.


The question of redisuicting the
wards of the city is one that has
been brought before the city coun-
Gil several times since the adop-
ton of the city charter in April
1891. Section OF the charter then
adopted, and which has not since
been subjected to criticism or sug'
gestion of an amendment, reads as
| follows:


The city council shall have the
power to redistrict the city into
